ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general congee entry (FOODON_00002572) but no specific milkfish belly congee entry. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers rice porridge/congee as a category. No specific milkfish belly congee code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for milkfish belly congee. SNOMED CT does not index this specific dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Taiwan milkfish belly gourmet porridge is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous portion of slow-cooked rice congee with milkfish belly pieces, typically served as a main meal.

nutrient_source.title: Fish porridge with milkfish (bangus) — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Milkfish, raw (NDB 15209) and Rice porridge/congee (NDB 20051), combined in approximate proportions (30% fish, 65% cooked rice porridge, 5% condiments)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Taiwan milkfish belly congee exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for raw milkfish (NDB 15209: protein ~18.9 g/100g raw, fat ~6.6 g/100g raw) and cooked plain rice porridge/congee (NDB 20051: ~25 kcal/100g, ~0.5 g protein, ~0.1 g fat, ~5.6 g carbohydrate per 100g). Dish composition estimated at approximately 30% milkfish belly (cooked, ~20% weight loss), 65% cooked rice congee, 5% condiments (ginger, spring onion, sesame oil). Final per-100g values represent the blended dish as served. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Rice congee / rice porridge GI — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Taiwan milkfish belly congee. GI of 57 is based on the analogous food 'rice congee' (GI ~57, medium) from the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables of GI values (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The fish component does not contribute glycemic carbohydrates and does not materially alter the GI of the rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 27.2) / 100 = 15.5, rounded to 15.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish stock and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 400 g serving provides approximately 1120 mg sodium, which represents nearly half the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index from rice congee base
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 57 (medium) and a glycemic load of ~15.6 per serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair this dish with non-starchy vegetables or additional protein to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from milkfish belly
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Milkfish belly is a source of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, which are cardioprotective; the dish has a moderate fat content (~2.8 g/100g) with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio, making it a reasonable choice for cardiovascular health when sodium is managed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taiwan Milkfish Belly Gourmet Porridge is a Teochew-style rice congee dish featuring the fatty, boneless belly cut of milkfish (Chanos chanos), known locally in Singapore as 'bangus' or 'shīmù yú' (虱目魚). The dish is slow-cooked by simmering white rice in fish stock or water until the grains break down into a smooth, creamy congee base, into which generous pieces of milkfish belly are gently poached. The milkfish belly is prized for its rich, buttery texture, high omega-3 content, and naturally sweet flavour. Seasonings typically include ginger slices, spring onion, white pepper, and a drizzle of sesame oil. The dish is served hot and is commonly eaten as a main meal at breakfast, lunch, or dinner in Singaporean Chinese seafood soup restaurants. Nutritionally, it provides moderate protein (~6.2 g/100g) from the fish, moderate carbohydrates (~6.8 g/100g) from the rice base, and beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fats. The sodium content is moderate to high depending on the stock used. The glycemic index is approximately 57 (medium), driven by the rice congee base. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults but requires sodium monitoring for hypertensive individuals and portion awareness for those managing Type 2 Diabetes. The omega-3 fatty acids from milkfish belly support cardiovascular health. This dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in milkfish belly porridge?
Is Taiwan milkfish porridge good for diabetics?
Is milkfish belly porridge high in sodium?
What are the omega-3 benefits of milkfish?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Taiwan milkfish belly congee was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for raw milkfish and cooked rice congee in estimated dish proportions. GI is based on the analogous food 'rice congee' from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the estimation methodology.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for milkfish (NDB 15209) and rice congee (NDB 20051) in approximate dish proportions (30% cooked milkfish belly, 65% cooked rice congee, 5% condiments). Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy milkfish or rice congee entries at the level of precision required for this composite dish. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported with sufficient precision in the source data for this composite dish. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy rice congee entry and the composite value cannot be reliably estimated. Trans fat is 0.0 as milkfish and plain rice congee contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy source data. Sodium estimate of 280 mg/100g reflects a moderately seasoned restaurant-style preparation with fish stock; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant. The milkfish belly cut is specifically the fatty ventral belly strip, which is higher in fat and omega-3 content than the dorsal fillet. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on rice congee from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Glycemic load per serving = (57 × 27.2 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 15.5, reported as 15.6.
